statement: I only read news that agrees with what I already believe — the rest feels untrustworthy.
explanation: Sources get dismissed for disagreeing, not because they're wrong.
reflectionQuestion: When did you last listen to a view you disagreed with — on purpose?
whyHumansDoThis: We lean toward what fits what we already think. It feels safer and easier — but it can narrow what we're willing to hear.
learningObjective: Learn that seeking confirming evidence feels natural but can limit better decisions.

statement: After I bought a stock, I only followed analysts who said it would rise — and ignored warnings about debt.
explanation: After deciding, only the good news gets noticed.
reflectionQuestion: After you decide something, do you look for doubts — or proof you were right?
whyHumansDoThis: Once we've committed, we want to feel we made the right call. Doubt is uncomfortable — so we often skip it.
learningObjective: Learn to actively look for disconfirming evidence after making decisions, not just before.

statement: My feed is full of posts I agree with — so it feels like everyone thinks the same way.
explanation: A curated feed gets mistaken for how most people think.
reflectionQuestion: Does your feed challenge you, or mostly agree with you?
whyHumansDoThis: We engage more with what we agree with. Platforms show us more of it — and the loop can feel like reality.
learningObjective: Learn how confirmation bias interacts with algorithms to create feedback loops that feel like reality.

statement: A house is listed at $500k. Even if that's high, buyers still haggle around that number.
explanation: The listing price becomes the anchor for every offer.
reflectionQuestion: Has a first price ever shaped what felt 'fair' to you?
whyHumansDoThis: The first number we see sticks. We adjust from there — but rarely far enough.
learningObjective: Learn to recognize when a starting number is shaping your judgment instead of the actual value.

statement: A jacket was $300, now $150 — feels like a steal, even if $150 is still a lot.
explanation: The old price makes the discount feel bigger than it is.
reflectionQuestion: Next 'sale' tag you see — what's the item actually worth?
whyHumansDoThis: We focus on the drop, not the real value. The starting price quietly pulls our judgment.
learningObjective: Learn to evaluate the actual value of something rather than the discount from an arbitrary starting price.

statement: In a job interview, they ask for my salary first — and that number ends up anchoring the whole negotiation.
explanation: The first number spoken becomes the reference for everything after.
reflectionQuestion: Have you ever felt stuck near a number you said early on?
whyHumansDoThis: Early numbers are hard to let go of — even when new info suggests we should move on.
learningObjective: Learn that anchors set early in a conversation can quietly control the entire negotiation.

statement: After a few plane-crash stories in the news, flying feels terrifying — even though stats say driving is riskier.
explanation: Vivid stories feel more common than they are.
reflectionQuestion: Any fear you have that might come from memorable stories, not stats?
whyHumansDoThis: Easy-to-recall events feel more likely. Our brain uses memory as a shortcut.
learningObjective: Learn that memorable events are not always common events.

statement: I saw one viral post about a rare vaccine side effect — and it stuck more than the disease risks I ignored.
explanation: One vivid story outweighed the broader data.
reflectionQuestion: On health risks, do you check the data or trust the story that sticks?
whyHumansDoThis: Emotional stories lodge in memory. Dry statistics often don't.
learningObjective: Learn to seek base-rate statistics rather than relying on memorable anecdotes.

statement: After a friend's house was burgled, burglaries suddenly feel common here — even though crime has been falling.
explanation: One close story outweighed the wider trend.
reflectionQuestion: Has one person's story ever changed how safe you feel?
whyHumansDoThis: Stories from people we know feel real and relevant — more than numbers on a page.
learningObjective: Learn to distinguish between a personally vivid story and an actual trend.

statement: A colleague was late to the meeting. Everyone assumed they're lazy — not knowing their car broke down.
explanation: Character got blamed before circumstances were considered.
reflectionQuestion: Next time someone's late — what situation might explain it?
whyHumansDoThis: We don't see others' full context, so we fill the gap with personality labels.
learningObjective: Learn to consider circumstances before judging character.

statement: Someone cut me off in traffic. I thought 'what a jerk' — without wondering if they had an emergency.
explanation: The action was blamed on character, not situation.
reflectionQuestion: Have you ever driven badly because of your own bad day?
whyHumansDoThis: It's faster to judge a stranger's character than imagine their unseen story.
learningObjective: Learn that the same behavior looks different depending on whether you know the situation behind it.

statement: A friend cancelled last minute again. I decided they're unreliable — maybe it was just a run of bad luck.
explanation: One label stuck before a real pattern was clear.
reflectionQuestion: How many times before you'd call something a pattern, not bad luck?
whyHumansDoThis: One clear story feels easier than holding uncertainty over time.
learningObjective: Learn to separate a single incident from an established pattern before judging someone's character.

statement: I passed because I'm smart — but failed last time because the questions were unfair.
explanation: Success went to me; failure went to outside factors.
reflectionQuestion: Your last win and loss — did you explain them the same way?
whyHumansDoThis: Crediting ourselves for wins and blaming outside forces for losses protects how we feel about ourselves.
learningObjective: Learn to apply the same standard of explanation to both your successes and failures.

statement: We won because of great strategy. We lost because of a bad referee.
explanation: The win was ours; the loss was someone else's fault.
reflectionQuestion: A loss you blamed entirely on something outside your control?
whyHumansDoThis: Keeping a positive self-image feels better than sitting with our own shortcomings.
learningObjective: Learn to evaluate wins and losses with the same level of scrutiny.

statement: A manager took credit when a project worked — and blamed the team when a similar one failed.
explanation: Success was personal; failure was shifted away.
reflectionQuestion: When your project fails — how much do you own versus blame others?
whyHumansDoThis: Protecting our sense of competence is easier than sharing responsibility for failure.
learningObjective: Learn to look for your own contribution to both successes and failures, not just the convenient one.

statement: I keep holding a failing stock because I've already put so much into it — hoping it'll bounce back.
explanation: Past money spent is driving the choice, not future prospects.
reflectionQuestion: If you were deciding fresh today, would you still hold it?
whyHumansDoThis: Walking away feels like admitting a mistake. Staying avoids that pain — even when it costs more.
learningObjective: Learn to evaluate decisions based on future outcomes, not past investment.

statement: I've spent five years in this relationship. I can't leave now — even though I'm unhappy.
explanation: Time already spent is treated as a reason to stay.
reflectionQuestion: Would you start this relationship today, knowing what you know?
whyHumansDoThis: Leaving can feel like wasted years were wasted for nothing. That's painful.
learningObjective: Learn that past time invested doesn't change what's the best choice going forward.

statement: We keep eating a mediocre meal we already paid for — even though we could stop.
explanation: Money already spent shouldn't change whether we keep eating.
reflectionQuestion: Does having paid change how much you 'should' finish?
whyHumansDoThis: We mix up 'already spent' with 'what to do next' — even when they're separate decisions.
learningObjective: Learn to separate a past, unrecoverable cost from the decision in front of you right now.

statement: Nine compliments and one criticism in my review — and I obsessed over the criticism all week.
explanation: One negative note outweighed nine positive ones.
reflectionQuestion: Last feedback you got — praise or criticism stuck more?
whyHumansDoThis: Bad news used to signal danger. Our brains still give it extra weight.
learningObjective: Learn to consciously weigh positive and negative feedback more evenly.

statement: News runs more bad stories than good ones — because negative headlines get more clicks.
explanation: Outlets and audiences both lean toward negative news.
reflectionQuestion: Do you click negative headlines more than positive ones?
whyHumansDoThis: Threats and bad news pull our attention harder than good news.
learningObjective: Learn to notice when media incentives are exploiting a psychological bias you already have.

statement: One critical comment from my partner stuck more than months of compliments.
explanation: A single negative remark dominated the emotional memory.
reflectionQuestion: One comment that overshadowed a lot of good moments?
whyHumansDoThis: Negative moments are processed more deeply and remembered more vividly.
learningObjective: Learn to actively track the ratio of positive to negative moments, rather than trusting your gut feeling.

statement: A polished, articulate candidate seemed competent at the technical work too — without any proof.
explanation: A good impression in one area spilled into another.
reflectionQuestion: Assumed someone was good at their job because they seemed confident?
whyHumansDoThis: One overall impression is easier than judging each skill separately.
learningObjective: Learn to evaluate specific skills on their own evidence, not on general impressions.

statement: A celebrity endorsed a health product — so fans assumed it works, because they like the celebrity.
explanation: Feelings about the person transferred to the product.
reflectionQuestion: Would you trust the claim as much from a stranger?
whyHumansDoThis: We pass trust from people we like to things they're linked with.
learningObjective: Learn to separate your feelings about a person from the evidence for their claims.

statement: A charismatic colleague got promoted fast — and people assumed all their ideas were good, untested ones included.
explanation: Likeability stretched to ideas without evidence.
reflectionQuestion: How do you separate liking someone from trusting their untested ideas?
whyHumansDoThis: A strong first impression is hard to update — even when proof is missing.
learningObjective: Learn that likeability and idea quality are two separate things, even if they feel linked.

statement: A new hire was late on day one. Colleagues decided they're unreliable and probably not hardworking.
explanation: One slip became a whole personality judgment.
reflectionQuestion: One early mistake that colored how you saw someone?
whyHumansDoThis: One flaw gets simplified into 'this person is generally bad' instead of staying specific.
learningObjective: Learn to keep a single flaw separate from your overall judgment of a person's other qualities.

statement: Someone mispronounced a word in a talk — and the audience doubted whether they know the topic at all.
explanation: A small slip was stretched into a competence judgment.
reflectionQuestion: Judged someone's expertise over an unrelated small mistake?
whyHumansDoThis: Our brain wants one consistent story — and one flaw builds a negative one fast.
learningObjective: Learn to judge competence based on relevant evidence, not unrelated small errors.

statement: A public figure got one fact wrong — and viewers dismissed everything else they said, even the solid parts.
explanation: One error discredited the whole message.
reflectionQuestion: When someone errs once, do you dismiss everything they say?
whyHumansDoThis: One clear mistake feels like reliable evidence — even when it isn't about everything else.
learningObjective: Learn to evaluate each claim on its own evidence rather than by association with an unrelated error.

statement: Success books interview winning entrepreneurs — not the many who tried the same thing and failed.
explanation: We only hear from survivors, so strategies look more reliable than they are.
reflectionQuestion: Reading a success story — do you ask who tried the same and failed?
whyHumansDoThis: Failures are quieter, less visible, and less fun to talk about.
learningObjective: Learn to actively look for the invisible failures behind visible success stories.

statement: 'These five habits made CEOs successful' — with no check on failed CEOs who had the same habits.
explanation: Without a comparison group, the link to success is shaky.
reflectionQuestion: Without asking who failed, can you trust why someone succeeded?
whyHumansDoThis: Success stories are celebrated. Quiet failures disappear from view.
learningObjective: Learn to ask for the comparison group before trusting a claim about what causes success.

statement: Everyone still in the course says it's easy — but the people who struggled already dropped out.
explanation: Only survivors are in the sample you're hearing from.
reflectionQuestion: Whose voices might be missing from the feedback you hear?
whyHumansDoThis: We hear from who's left — and forget who already walked away.
learningObjective: Learn to consider who is missing from a sample before drawing a conclusion.

statement: After a stock crashed, I said 'I knew that would happen' — though I'd bought more the week before.
explanation: Memory of what we believed shifted to match the outcome.
reflectionQuestion: Claimed you 'knew' something — despite acting the opposite before?
whyHumansDoThis: Once we know the ending, it feels inevitable — and our memory bends to match.
learningObjective: Learn to check your past predictions honestly instead of assuming you always saw it coming.

statement: After the loss, I insisted 'I knew we'd lose' — though I'd predicted a win the day before.
explanation: Recollection of the prediction was rewritten after the fact.
reflectionQuestion: Could you find what you actually predicted — versus what you claim now?
whyHumansDoThis: We rebuild past beliefs to fit what we know now.
learningObjective: Learn that memory of a prediction is not a reliable substitute for the prediction itself.

statement: After a project failed, the team said 'the risks were obvious from the start' — though nobody flagged them in planning.
explanation: Risks look obvious only after failure is known.
reflectionQuestion: Were the 'obvious' risks written down before the outcome?
whyHumansDoThis: Knowing how it ended makes the path feel more predictable than it was.
learningObjective: Learn to check documented predictions rather than trusting a reconstructed memory of what was 'obvious.'

statement: I assume most people share my political view — because everyone in my circle does.
explanation: My bubble was mistaken for the whole population.
reflectionQuestion: How would you check if most people agree — beyond your circle?
whyHumansDoThis: We're surrounded by similar people, so their views feel like everyone's.
learningObjective: Learn to distinguish between the views of your social circle and the views of the wider population.

statement: I posted a hot take online and got slammed — I'd assumed 'everyone thinks this.'
explanation: My bubble felt like consensus.
reflectionQuestion: Online backlash that surprised you because you thought your view was common?
whyHumansDoThis: Our own beliefs feel normal — so we assume others share them.
learningObjective: Learn that a view feeling normal to you doesn't mean it's widely shared.

statement: Two people agreed in a meeting — so the manager assumed the whole team supports the policy.
explanation: A vocal few stood in for everyone.
reflectionQuestion: When few speak up — what can you really conclude about the rest?
whyHumansDoThis: Loud agreement is easy to notice. Silent disagreement is invisible.
learningObjective: Learn to distinguish between a vocal minority and true group consensus.

statement: Same mistake from my department gets a pass — from another department, it's a serious problem.
explanation: Group membership changed how the mistake was judged.
reflectionQuestion: Judge mistakes from 'your people' more gently than from others?
whyHumansDoThis: We extend more trust and leniency to people we see as 'us.'
learningObjective: Learn to apply the same standard of judgment regardless of group membership.

statement: Our player's foul was accidental. Their player's identical foul was dirty play.
explanation: Same action, different story — depending on the team.
reflectionQuestion: Would you see it the same way if the teams were swapped?
whyHumansDoThis: Loyalty shapes how we read ambiguous actions — we favor our side.
learningObjective: Learn to test your judgment by imagining the same action from the 'other side.'

statement: I excuse a scandal from my party — but I'm outraged by the same thing from the other side.
explanation: Group allegiance changed the moral judgment.
reflectionQuestion: Would this bother you as much from your own side?
whyHumansDoThis: Protecting our group's reputation can feel like protecting part of ourselves.
learningObjective: Learn to notice when group loyalty, not the facts, is driving your judgment.

statement: Marketing sees lots of personalities in marketing — but assumes everyone in engineering is the same.
explanation: Diversity inside my group; sameness assumed outside it.
reflectionQuestion: Which groups do you see as diverse — and which do you lump together?
whyHumansDoThis: We know individuals in our group. Other groups we only see from a distance.
learningObjective: Learn that unfamiliarity, not actual similarity, often drives the perception that 'they're all the same.'

statement: I've never lived there — but I'm sure people from that country all think the same on this issue.
explanation: Limited exposure became a uniform stereotype.
reflectionQuestion: How much real exposure do you have to the group you're generalizing?
whyHumansDoThis: Less detail about a group means broader, simpler assumptions.
learningObjective: Learn to question generalizations about groups you don't know well.

statement: A headline says 'young people today' all think one way — but we'd never describe our own generation that simply.
explanation: A diverse group was flattened into one attitude.
reflectionQuestion: Would you accept this generalization about a group you're in?
whyHumansDoThis: Big categories are easier to write and think about than real diversity.
learningObjective: Learn to notice when a large group is being described as if it were one single, uniform entity.

statement: '90% survival rate' sounds great. '10% mortality rate' sounds scary. Same treatment.
explanation: Identical stats, different emotional frame.
reflectionQuestion: A decision where wording changed how you felt?
whyHumansDoThis: We react to gain vs loss framing before we sit with the numbers.
learningObjective: Learn to translate framed information into neutral terms before deciding.

statement: '90% fat free' sells better than '10% fat' — same product on the label.
explanation: Positive wording beat equivalent negative wording.
reflectionQuestion: Chosen a product because of the label, not the content?
whyHumansDoThis: Positive-sounding words trigger a warmer gut reaction — facts aside.
learningObjective: Learn to check the actual numbers behind marketing language, not just the wording used.

statement: One side says a policy saves 200 lives. The other says it fails to save 100 — same outcome, different frame.
explanation: Same numbers, opposite emotional pull.
reflectionQuestion: Do you flip the frame to see if the numbers still hold?
whyHumansDoThis: Communicators pick the frame that triggers the feeling they want.
learningObjective: Learn to reframe a statistic in the opposite direction before forming an opinion about it.

statement: Losing $1,000 hurts more than gaining $1,000 feels good — same amount.
explanation: Losses weigh heavier than equal gains.
reflectionQuestion: Would you take a 50/50 bet to win or lose $100?
whyHumansDoThis: Avoiding loss mattered more for survival than chasing equal rewards.
learningObjective: Learn to notice when the fear of losing is outweighing a rational evaluation of the actual stakes.

statement: In a negotiation, I'll fight harder to keep what I have than to gain something new of equal value.
explanation: Losing what we have feels worse than gaining the same amount.
reflectionQuestion: Last negotiation — fighting harder to keep or to gain?
whyHumansDoThis: Losses are felt more intensely than equivalent gains — often about twice as much.
learningObjective: Learn to evaluate gains and losses of equal size with the same level of effort.

statement: I keep a gym membership I never use — canceling feels like losing something.
explanation: Avoiding the feeling of loss beats the logic of stopping payment.
reflectionQuestion: Keeping something unused — value, or just fear of losing it?
whyHumansDoThis: 'Losing' something — even unused — can outweigh the clear case for letting go.
learningObjective: Learn to separate the feeling of loss from the actual future costs and benefits of a decision.

statement: I smoke but tell myself I'm less likely to get sick than other smokers — no real reason why.
explanation: 'It won't happen to me' without evidence.
reflectionQuestion: A risk you've downplayed for yourself but not for others?
whyHumansDoThis: Believing we're the exception reduces anxiety and keeps us moving.
learningObjective: Learn to apply the same risk assessment to yourself that you would to someone else in your situation.

statement: We always estimate projects will finish early — despite a long history of running late.
explanation: Past delays get ignored for a hopeful new estimate.
reflectionQuestion: Does planning use real history — or just this project's best case?
whyHumansDoThis: We picture the best outcome for our own plans and discount past slip-ups.
learningObjective: Learn to use historical data, not just hopeful intuition, when estimating future outcomes.

statement: I'm not saving for retirement — I'll 'figure it out later' and assume I'll earn more than stats suggest.
explanation: A rosier-than-average future assumed without proof.
reflectionQuestion: Is your plan based on realistic odds — or a hoped-for best case?
whyHumansDoThis: An optimistic future feels easier than facing uncertain odds.
learningObjective: Learn to plan around realistic, evidence-based expectations rather than a hoped-for best case.

statement: I stayed in my company's default retirement plan — even though a better one needed just one form.
explanation: No action beat a slightly better option.
reflectionQuestion: A default choice you've never actually reconsidered?
whyHumansDoThis: Sticking with now takes no effort. Change takes a decision.
learningObjective: Learn to periodically re-evaluate default choices instead of assuming they're still the best option.

statement: We keep outdated software because switching feels risky — though the new system would clearly save time and money.
explanation: Familiar beat a rational case for change.
reflectionQuestion: Something at work you keep because 'that's how it's always been'?
whyHumansDoThis: Change means uncertainty. Staying put feels safe and predictable.
learningObjective: Learn to evaluate the current approach against alternatives on their actual merits, not just familiarity.

statement: I've kept my phone's default settings for years — assuming the manufacturer picked the best ones.
explanation: Default was treated as best without checking.
reflectionQuestion: Default settings you've never questioned — phone, habits, anything?
whyHumansDoThis: Defaults feel like a recommendation. Changing them takes mental effort.
learningObjective: Learn to distinguish between 'default' and 'best,' since they aren't always the same thing.

statement: After one online course, I felt like an expert in a field professionals study for years.
explanation: A little learning created a lot of confidence.
reflectionQuestion: A skill where early confidence later met how much you didn't know?
whyHumansDoThis: Early learning skips the part where you discover how much is still ahead.
learningObjective: Learn that early confidence in a new skill often reflects unawareness of its full complexity, not real mastery.

statement: As a beginner, I'm sure I could beat a chess grandmaster with the right strategy.
explanation: Limited experience hid how wide the skill gap really is.
reflectionQuestion: How do you find out how good you really are at something new?
whyHumansDoThis: Without expert knowledge, it's hard to see what expert skill actually looks like.
learningObjective: Learn to seek outside, expert feedback to calibrate your own skill level accurately.

statement: A few articles online — and I felt more sure about my symptoms than the doctor with thousands of cases.
explanation: Surface reading felt like real expertise.
reflectionQuestion: When is your own research enough — and when do you need a pro?
whyHumansDoThis: A little information can feel complete when depth is still missing.
learningObjective: Learn to recognize the difference between surface-level familiarity and deep expertise.

statement: I trusted a diet tip from my doctor — even though it wasn't their area of expertise.
explanation: General authority stretched to a specific claim.
reflectionQuestion: Does their authority actually match this specific advice?
whyHumansDoThis: Titles and status become shortcuts for trust — instead of checking each claim.
learningObjective: Learn to check whether someone's authority is actually relevant to the specific claim they're making.

statement: I didn't question a flawed plan because a senior executive proposed it.
explanation: Rank stood in for a sound argument.
reflectionQuestion: Gone along with an idea mainly because of who suggested it?
whyHumansDoThis: Deferring to authority feels safer than scrutinizing the idea itself.
learningObjective: Learn to evaluate ideas on their own merits, separate from the status of the person proposing them.

statement: An actor who played a doctor on TV comments on real medicine — and viewers partly trust them because of the role.
explanation: A fictional role lent unearned credibility.
reflectionQuestion: Would you trust this take as much if you didn't know who was speaking?
whyHumansDoThis: Familiarity with an authority-like role can feel like real expertise.
learningObjective: Learn to check whether a person's actual expertise, not their perceived role, supports their claim.
